subject: Internal medicine
title: Fever
preambleMarkdown:
# Fever



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever is a symptom, syndrome, or diagnostic approach topic in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Fever to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, progressive, or episodic presentation.
- Localized versus systemic syndrome.
- Stable outpatient problem versus emergency presentation.
- Primary disease, complication, medication effect, functional disorder, or psychosocial contributor.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Fever depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Fever.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Fever disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Fever should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Fever first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Fever, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define onset, timing, triggers, severity, associated symptoms, prior episodes, medication exposures, and functional impact.
- The same symptom can represent benign disease or a time-sensitive emergency.
- Vital signs and general appearance often decide whether broad testing or immediate stabilization comes first.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Fever, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Fever presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Fever with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Fever is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Fever should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize life-threatening causes, treat pain or distress, and choose tests that separate dangerous from common causes.
- Treat the identified cause rather than the symptom label alone.
- Arrange follow-up and safety-net instructions when initial evaluation is nondiagnostic.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Fever is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Fever, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Fever may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Fever patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Fever, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Fever in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Fever, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Fever does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Fever may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Fever, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Fever as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- A symptom is not a diagnosis.
- First rule out unstable and cannot-miss causes.
- The next best test is the one that changes the next decision.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Fever draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Other Infections

_Source lines: 14455-14894._

```text
VIII. OTHER INFECTIONS
A. Parasitic infections
1. Discussion. The incidence of parasitic disease is increasing in the United States
due to immigration from other countries, increased travel, and an increase in
immunosuppression caused by HIV.

2. Clinical entities
a. Ascariasis is caused by Ascaris lumbricoides. The larvae hatch from
ingested eggs and migrate through the bloodstream to the lungs, causing
fever, cough , dyspnea , hemoptysis, and eosinophilia.

b. Pinworm infection is caused by Enterobius vermicularis (Figure 6-7). The
eggs hatch in the cecum, appendix, ileum, and ascending colon. The gravid
F I G U R E
6-7 	Pinworm.

(Courtesy of Lawrence B. Stack, MD.)

178 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Infectious Disease Emergencies
female migrates to the anus (usually at night), where it causes an intense
pruritus (pruritus ani) .

c. Hookworm infection is caused by Necator americanus, which is prevalent in
Southern climates. Infection is associated with human waste used as fertilizer
and the lack of shoes and latrines. Because each worm feeds on 0.03 to 0.2 mL
of blood per day, chronic infection leads to chronic anemia, especially in chil-
dren. Patients may present with a cough, low-grade fever, abdominal pain,
diarrhea, weakness, weight loss, guaiac-positive stools, and eosinophilia.

d. Threadworm infection is caused by Strongyloides stercoralis, which infests
the mucosa of the small intestine. The parasite invades the body by pen-
etrating the skin, leading to pruritus and an erythematous rash (cutaneous
larval migrans). As the worms migrate through the lungs to the gastrointes-
tinal tract, they also cause cough, dyspnea, and pneumonia. After they reach
the intestine, they produce abdominal pain and bloody mucoid diarrhea.

Fatalities have occurred in elderly and immunocompromised patients.

e. Whipworm infection is caused by Trichuris trichiura, a parasite most often
found in rural communities in the United States. Children may become in-
fected when playing in soil contaminated by ova. The adult worm resides in
the cecum, causing anorexia, insomnia, abdominal pain, fever, flatulence, di-
arrhea, weight loss, pruritus, eosinophilia, and microcytic hypochromic ane-
mia. Trichuris infestation can result in colitis and rectal prolapse in children.

f. Trichinellosis is caused by Trichinella. Transmission is by the ingestion of in-
fected pork, beef, or walrus meat. Symptoms depend on the number of worms
ingested, the number of larvae produced, and the site of invasion, although
the primary lesions are in striated muscle; clinical manifestations include acute
myocarditis, nonsuppurative meningitis, bronchopneumonia, or catarrhal
enteritis. Patients may present with nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, fever, ur-
ticaria, periorbital edema (which is pathognomonic), splinter hemorrhages,
myalgia, muscle spasms, a stiff neck, headache, and psychiatric disorders.

g. Schistosomiasis is caused by Schistosoma. The parasite penetrates the skin,
creating a maculopapular rash, and the adult parasites then reside in the
venous system.

i. Acute disease (Katayama fever) is severe but rarely seen; lymphade-
nopathy and hepatosplenomegaly are characteristic.

ii. Chronic disease. More typically, patients present in the chronic state
with granulomas in the liver (leading to portal hypertension) and blad-
der (leading to obstructive hydroureter). Patients may present with
diarrhea, abdominal pain, melena, hepatosplenomegaly, hematemesis,
ascites, and liver failure. With S. haematobium infection, dysuria and
hematuria may be found.

h. Tapeworm infections are caused by Taenia solium (pork tapeworm), Taenia
saginata (beef tapeworm), and Diphyllobothrium latum (fish tapeworm).

i. Pork tapeworm is indigenous to Central America and the Middle East. T.

solium larvae may encyst in the subcutaneous tissues, eye, brain, and heart,
causing seizures, myocarditis, periorbital edema, and sometimes morbidity.

ii. Beef tapeworm is more common. Adult worms live in the small intes-
tine. Infected patients can present with nausea, vomiting, headache,
abdominal pain, pruritus, constipation, diarrhea, and intestinal
obstruction, or they may be asymptomatic.

iii. Fish tapeworm. Consumption of raw or undercooked fish (e.g., sushi;
sashimi; pickled, salted, or smoked fish) is the most common method
of transmission to humans. The parasite resides in the intestine and
absorbs vitamin B12, causing a pernicious anemia.

i. Amebiasis is caused by E. histolytica. The amoebae inhabit the cecum
and large intestine, causing ulcers and diffuse inflammation that generally
mimic ulcerative colitis. A total of 50% of infected patients are asymp-
tomatic; the remaining 50% may experience nausea, vomiting, anorexia,
diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain, and leukocytosis. Rarely, amebiasis can
develop in the liver and produce an abscess.

I N F E C T I O U S D I S E A S E E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	179

Infectious Disease Emergencies
j. Giardiasis is caused by Giardia lamblia and is the most common para-
sitic intestinal infection in the United States. Cysts are ingested in fecally
contaminated water or are passed by hand-to-mouth transmission; once
ingested, the parasite inhabits the host's duodenum and upper jejunum.

Symptoms include explosive, foul-smelling diarrhea, flatus, abdominal
distention, fatigue, fever, weight loss, and malaise.

k. Trypanosomiasis is caused by Trypanosoma species.

i. T. cruzi, the American variety, causes Chagas disease. The acute phase
of illness can last 2 to 3 months and consists of fever, headache,
anorexia, conjunctivitis, and myocarditis. Infants can develop menin-
goencephalitis, and heart involvement can lead to congestive heart
failure and ventricular aneurysms. The organism can attack the myen-
teric plexus of the gastrointestinal tract, resulting in megacolon.

ii. T. brucei rhodesiense and T. brucei gambiense, the African varieties,
cause sleeping sickness.

3. Differential diagnoses include bacterial or viral infection, collagen vascular
disease, and neoplasia.

4. Evaluation is mainly by fecal analysis and serology.

a. Ascariasis is diagnosed by finding eggs of the adult worm in the stool
sample. Serologic tests (e.g., bentonite flocculation, ELISA, indirect hemag-
glutination) may also be useful.

b. Pinworm infection is diagnosed by finding eggs or worms on a cellophane
tape swab of the anus. Accuracy is improved by examination and testing in
the early morning.

c. Hookworm infection is diagnosed by finding ova in the stool sample.

d. Threadworm infection is diagnosed by finding ova in a stool sample or a
duodenal aspirate.

e. Whipworm infection is diagnosed by finding ova in the stool sample.

f. Trichinellosis is characterized by leukocytosis, eosinophilia, elevated serum
creatine phosphokinase levels, and nonspecific electrocardiogram charges.

The diagnosis can be confirmed with a latex agglutination skin test and a
complement fixation or bentonite flocculation test (available from the Centers
for Disease Control). ELISA is very sensitive and specific after the third week,
and biopsy of tender muscle may be helpful after the fourth week of infection.

g. Schistosomiasis is diagnosed by observing eggs in the feces or on rectal biopsy.

h. Tapeworm infection, amebiasis, and giardiasis are diagnosed by ova and
parasite stool examination and ELISA.

i. Chagas disease is characterized by anemia, leukocytosis, an elevated eryth-
rocyte sedimentation rate, and electrocardiogram changes (PR- and R-wave
changes, heart block, arrhythmias).

## 5. Therapy

a. Ascariasis, pinworms, and hookworms are treated with mebendazole or
pyrantel pamoate.

b. Whipworms and trichinellosis are treated with mebendazole .

c. Threadworms are treated with thiabendazole.

d. Schistosomiasis and tapeworms are treated with praziquantel .

e. Amebiasis is treated with metronidazole followed by iodoquinol .

f. Giardiasis can be treated with quinacrine or metronidazole .

g. Chagas disease is treated with nifurtimox.

6. Disposition. Most parasitic infections are treated on an outpatient basis,
although complications such as dehydration secondary to gastritis or
myocardial involvement may require inpatient therapy for supportive care.

B. Tick-borne disease
1. Lyme disease
a. Discussion. Lyme disease, caused by the spirochete B. burgdorferi, is the
most frequently transmitted tick-borne disease. It is prevalent in 33 of the
50 states, with the highest incidence in the Northeast. The majority of cases
occur in the late spring and late summer.

180 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Infectious Disease Emergencies
Lyme Disease Stages
1. Stage I is characterized by erythema chronicum migrans (an annular lesion) and flu-like symptoms
of fever, chills, headache, malaise, and weakness. Symptoms appear 3 to 32 days after the tick bite.

2. Stage II begins 4 weeks later. Ten percent of untreated patients develop neurologic disorders
(e.g., headache, meningoencephalitis, facial nerve palsy, radiculoneuropathy) and cardiac disease
(e.g., first-, second-, or third-degree atrioventricular block).

3. Stage III occurs in 60% of patients with untreated Lyme disease several weeks or years after infection.

Patients develop migratory polyoligoarthritis that most often involves the knee, shoulder, and elbow.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	6-6
b. Clinical features. Lyme disease has three stages (see Clinical Pearl 6-6).

c. Differential diagnoses include viral syndromes, connective tissue disease,
Guillain-Barre syndrome, and rheumatoid arthritis.

d. Evaluation. Diagnosis is best made by careful history and physical
examination.

i. Laboratory studies. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate is usually
elevated and the lymphocyte count slightly decreased.

e. Treatment
i. Tick removal. If the patient presents with stage I symptoms, a careful
search for the tick should ensue. The tick is best removed by grasp-
ing the head with forceps and gently pulling it away from the skin (see
Clinical Pearl 6-7).

ii. Antibacterial therapy
a) Adults and nonpregnant women. Treatment is with doxycycline
(100 mg orally twice daily for 10 to 21 days).

b) Children, allergic patients, and pregnant women. Amoxicillin
(40 mg/kg/day three times daily), penicillin, or erythromycin (30 to
50 mg/kg/day five times daily for children) can be used.

c) Patients with stage II disease. For patients with severe neurologic or
cardiac symptoms, ceftriaxone (1 g intravenously every 12 hours for
10 to 14 days) is recommended.

f. Disposition. Lyme disease can usually be treated on an outpatient basis,
regardless of the stage of disease.

2. Rocky Mountain spotted fever
a. Discussion. Rocky Mountain spotted fever, caused by Rickettsia rickettsii, is
the second most common tick-borne disease. Rocky Mountain spotted fever
occurs primarily in the Northwest United States but has also been reported
in the Southern and Eastern states.

i. Incidence. There are 500 to 1,000 cases annually. Peak incidence occurs
from late spring to early fall.

b. Clinical features. Rocky Mountain spotted fever has a wide clinical spec-
trum. The triad of fever, headache, and rash is seen in 55% to 65% of
patients.

i. Initial symptoms are fever, followed by an erythematous, maculopapular,
blotchy rash that ultimately becomes petechial. The rash is present in
Prophylaxis for Lyme Disease
All inclusion criteria should be met.

1. Correct tick: identified as Ixodes
2. Correct time: !36 hours attached and engorged tick and/or '72 hours since removal
3. Correct location: endemic area % Northeast United States, some of Midwest and Pacific Northwest
If criteria met, doxycycline 200 mg by mouth once.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	6-7
I N F E C T I O U S D I S E A S E E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	181

Infectious Disease Emergencies
75% to 80% of patients, begins at the flexor surfaces of the wrists and
ankles, and spreads centripetally.

ii. Other symptoms are vomiting (66% of patients), myalgia (85% of
patients), cough (33% of patients), and signs of meningoencephalitic
involvement (25% of patients).

c. Evaluation. The diagnosis of Rocky Mountain spotted fever is difficult.

In addition to the physical examination and history, immunofluorescent
antibody staining or polymerase chain reaction can be performed on a
skin biopsy specimen obtained from an area of rash.

d. Therapy for Rocky Mountain spotted fever is tetracycline (10 to 70 mg/kg
given intravenously in one dose for 10 days for adults and children older
than 8 years) or chloramphenicol (80 mg/kg given intravenously in one
dose for children younger than 8 years).

e. Disposition. Patients usually require admission.

3. Tick paralysis
a. Discussion. Tick paralysis is a relatively uncommon tick-borne disease
resulting in an ascending paralysis.

i. Incidence. Incidence is highest in late spring to late summer and is
higher in girls than in boys. The mortality rate in untreated cases may
exceed 12%.

ii. Pathogenesis. Tick paralysis is believed to be caused by a venom
secreted from the female tick salivary glands during feeding-most
probably a neurotoxin that produces a block of the peripheral motor
nerve endplate, resulting in a failure of acetylcholine release at the
neuromuscular junction.

b. Clinical features. Symptoms of tick paralysis develop within 4 to 7 days
and consist of restlessness and paresthesias of the hands or feet. Within
1 to 2 days, the presenting symptoms are followed by a symmetric, ascend-
ing, flaccid paralysis accompanied by loss of deep tendon reflexes. Death
can result from respiratory paralysis.

c. Evaluation. Tick paralysis is diagnosed by symptoms and discovery of
the tick.

d. Therapy. Tick paralysis is treated by tick removal.

e. Disposition. Admission to the hospital may be required if the patient's
symptoms are severe.

4. Relapsing fever
a. Discussion. Relapsing fever is an uncommon acute recurrent febrile illness
caused by a spirochete of the Borrelia species. It is isolated to the Western
and Southwestern United States, with peak incidence in the summer months.

b. Clinical features. After an incubation period of 5 to 9 days, individuals
with relapsing fever may experience a febrile episode lasting 3 days,
followed by an afebrile period and a return of fever. The fever is
accompanied by chills, malaise, vomiting, headache, and myalgias. Spleno-
megaly (40% of patients), hepatomegaly (20% of patients), and neurologic
involvement (10% of patients) may also be noted.

c. Evaluation. There are no helpful serologic tests for relapsing fever, but
peripheral blood smears show spirochetes in up to 70% of patients. Clinical
presentation usually points to the diagnosis.

d. Therapy. Relapsing fever is treated with tetracycline (500 mg orally four
times daily for 10 days) or erythromycin (250 mg orally four times daily for
10 days).

e. Disposition. Admission to the hospital may be required if the patient's
symptoms are severe.

5. Q fever is caused by Coxiella burnetii and presents as a flu-like illness charac-
terized by fever, myalgias, headache, and cough. Q fever is diagnosed by com-
plement fixing antibody titers and clinical presentation. The flu-like syndrome
resolves spontaneously in 2 to 4 weeks, but treatment is with tetracycline.

Hospital admission may be required in the presence of severe symptoms.

182 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Infectious Disease Emergencies

## 6. Tularemia

a. Discussion. Tularemia is caused by the bacterium Francisella tularensis.

Although it was originally thought that tularemia was spread by rabbits and
rabbit meat, it is now recognized that ticks are the most frequent vector.

Peak incidence occurs in the summer months; most cases occur in the
Southern and Midwestern states.

b. Clinical features. Tularemia has two major presentations.

i. Ulceroglandular tularemia is the most common (occurring in approxi-
mately 50% of patients) and presents as lymphadenopathy, fever, and
reddened nodules that indurate and then ulcerate.

ii. Typhoidal tularemia presents with fever, chills, debility, abdominal pain,
diarrhea, anorexia, and weight loss.

c. Evaluation. Tularemia is diagnosed on the basis of history and physical
examination findings. Acute specific agglutination titers greater than 1:160
are diagnostic.

d. Therapy. Tularemia is treated with streptomycin (30 to 40 mg/kg given
in one dose for 4 to 7 days). Tetracycline (50 to 60 mg/kg given in four
divided doses daily for 14 days) is an alternative for patients who are
sensitive to streptomycin.

e. Disposition. Hospital admission is usually required.

## 7. Babesiosis

a. Discussion. Babesiosis is caused by Babesia, an intraerythrocytic protozoal
parasite that causes a malaria-like syndrome. The distribution is similar to
that of Lyme disease; in fact, the two entities have been seen concurrently
in the same patient. Babesiosis was originally reported in only splenecto-
mized individuals, but since 1969, there has been an increased incidence in
all patients.

b. Clinical features. Babesiosis has a broad range of clinical presentations,
from a brief febrile illness to severe disease characterized by hemolytic
anemia, hemoglobinuria, and death.

i. Fever, malaise, anorexia, and fatigue are almost universally present. Head-
ache and mild to moderate hemolytic anemia are also commonly seen.

ii. The physical examination is usually nonspecific, although splenomegaly
is seen in 40% of patients.

c. Evaluation. Thick and thin Giemsa-stained blood smears will usually show
intraerythrocytic organisms. A presumptive diagnosis can also be made
by using indirect immunofluorescent staining for antibody if the titers are
greater than 1:256.

d. Therapy
i. Antibacterial therapy. Babesiosis can be treated with clindamycin
(600 mg orally twice daily for adults; 20 to 30 mg/kg/day every 6 hours
for children) and quinine (650 mg orally twice daily for adults;
25 mg/kg/day every 8 hours for children) for 14 days.

ii. Exchange transfusion has been effective in severe cases.

e. Disposition. Hospital admission is indicated for patients with severe
disease.

8. Colorado tick fever
a. Discussion. Colorado tick fever is caused by an orbivirus of the family
Reoviridae. Only 200 cases are reported annually.

b. Clinical features. Patients develop the sudden onset of fever, headache,
lethargy, myalgias, and anorexia within 3 to 6 days of exposure.

c. Evaluation is by the clinical presentation and history.

d. Therapy. Colorado tick fever is a self-limited disease. Patients usually
recover in 3 weeks and treatment is supportive.

e. Disposition. Patients with severe symptoms may require hospital admission.
```
